skill.name: azure-ai-projects-dotnet
skill.description: Guides .NET developers through Azure AI Projects SDK usage for Azure AI Foundry projects, including agents, connections, datasets, deployments, evaluations, indexes, and related clients.
skill.summary: Build and manage Azure AI Foundry project workflows with the Azure AI Projects SDK for .NET.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution and blocker patterns, but review found they are Markdown backticks, ordinary SDK identifiers, and placeholder text in a documentation-only skill. The remaining risks are low: examples use Azure network services, read environment configuration, upload or delete project resources, and show credential retrieval that users must apply carefully.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown code fences and inline identifiers as Ruby or shell backtick execution. SKILL.md is a documentation file, and these locations are examples or tables rather than executable skill code.
low_findings.recommendation: No blocking action is required. Keep command examples clearly fenced as documentation and review any future executable scripts separately.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown fences, C# examples, tables, or inline type names. No Ruby source file or runtime backtick execution path exists in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Azure Endpoint and Documentation URLs Are Expected
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are an Azure AI Foundry endpoint placeholder, an Azure OpenAI URI reconstruction example, and reference links to NuGet, Microsoft Learn, and GitHub. They do not send data from the skill itself.
low_findings.recommendation: Publish with a normal network-use notice because generated application code may contact Azure and related Microsoft services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visibly placeholders or official documentation links. The skill is Markdown guidance and contains no executable network client controlled by the skill package.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Is Not Present
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptographic algorithm findings do not correspond to cryptographic API usage. Line 3 is metadata description text, and line 210 is a sample index description string.
low_findings.recommendation: No remediation is required for these lines. Reassess if future versions add hashing, encryption, or signing examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic calls at the reported locations. The scanner matched benign text in a Markdown document.

low_findings.title: URI Validation Is Not System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance finding points to an InvalidOperationException raised when a URI locator is invalid. This is input validation in a C# example, not host inspection or environment probing.
low_findings.recommendation: No security change is needed. Keep validation examples narrow and avoid adding host enumeration commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding example validates an Azure OpenAI connection URI. It does not run discovery commands or read local system metadata.

low_findings.title: Credential-Including Connection Example Needs Care
low_findings.description: The skill shows includeCredentials: true for retrieving a specific connection and later advises using it only when needed. This is legitimate SDK guidance, but users could expose secrets if they log or share returned connection details.
low_findings.recommendation: Keep the caution visible in generated guidance. Advise users not to print, persist, or commit returned credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential option is explicit and security-sensitive, but it appears in documentation with a limiting best-practice note. No exfiltration code or malicious destination is present.

content.value_statement: Azure AI Foundry project code can be hard to assemble across agents, datasets, deployments, and evaluations. This skill gives focused .NET SDK patterns for common project workflows.
actual_capabilities: Shows package installation commands for Azure.AI.Projects and related .NET SDK packages.
Demonstrates AIProjectClient setup with DefaultAzureCredential and project endpoints.
Provides examples for persistent agents, versioned agents, threads, runs, and cleanup.
Covers connections, deployments, datasets, indexes, evaluations, and Azure OpenAI chat clients.
Lists available agent tool classes and key SDK types for quick reference.
Includes practical best practices for async calls, polling, cleanup, pagination, and credential use.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not execute Azure operations by itself.
Version details may become outdated as Azure SDK packages change.
Examples require a configured Azure AI Foundry project and valid Azure credentials.
It does not replace official Azure documentation for production compliance requirements.

use_cases.title: Create a Foundry agent workflow
use_cases.description: Use the examples to create an agent, start a thread, run a request, read messages, and clean up resources.

use_cases.title: Manage project assets
use_cases.description: Reference SDK patterns for listing connections, checking deployments, uploading datasets, and managing indexes.

use_cases.title: Add evaluations to AI projects
use_cases.description: Use the evaluation examples to configure evaluators, run an evaluation, and review evaluation status.

prompt_templates.title: Set up a client
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to explain how to configure AIProjectClient for a .NET app using DefaultAzureCredential and PROJECT_ENDPOINT.

prompt_templates.title: Create an agent flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft the steps for creating a persistent agent, sending one user message, polling the run, and cleaning up.

prompt_templates.title: Plan project resource management
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline how my .NET service should list deployments, retrieve connections safely, upload a dataset, and create an index.

prompt_templates.title: Design a production pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my Azure AI Projects .NET design for agents, evaluations, async calls, pagination, cleanup, and credential handling.

output_examples.input: Explain how to start with Azure AI Projects in a .NET service.
output_examples.output: A concise setup checklist covering NuGet packages, PROJECT_ENDPOINT, DefaultAzureCredential, AIProjectClient, and the first SDK operation to test.

output_examples.input: Help me compare the SDK clients used by this package.
output_examples.output: A plain-language map of AIProjectClient, PersistentAgentsClient, ConnectionsClient, DatasetsClient, DeploymentsClient, EvaluationsClient, and IndexesClient.

output_examples.input: Review my plan for using connection credentials.
output_examples.output: A security-focused review that recommends retrieving credentials only when required and avoiding logs, commits, or shared outputs that contain secrets.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential and managed identity where possible for production authentication.
Prefer async SDK methods for network operations and handle pagination when listing resources.
Delete temporary threads, agents, files, datasets, and indexes when they are no longer needed.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode real Azure endpoints, connection names, or credentials in source control.
Do not request includeCredentials: true unless the workflow specifically requires returned secrets.
Do not ignore failed run states, request failures, or long polling loops in production services.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and examples for .NET code that users can adapt.

faq.question: Which Azure package is the main focus?
faq.answer: The main package is Azure.AI.Projects for .NET, with related Azure Identity and agent packages.

faq.question: Can it help with Azure AI Foundry agents?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes persistent agent and versioned agent workflows, including threads, runs, messages, and cleanup.

faq.question: Does it cover evaluations?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to configure an evaluator, create an evaluation, retrieve results, and list evaluations.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: Examples use DefaultAzureCredential and expect a configured Azure identity with access to the target project.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code for guided .NET implementation work.
